title: Two Pointers in Java
summary: Converging pointers (two-sum, palindrome), same-direction pointers (remove duplicates, fast/slow), Dutch National Flag 3-way partition, and interval merging.

questions.prompt: Converging two-pointer on a sorted array works because:
choices: Sorted order lets you decide which pointer to move based on whether the current sum is too large or too small
Only works for pairs summing to 0
questions.explanation: If sum < target, moving l right increases the sum. If sum > target, moving r left decreases it. Without sorted order this logic breaks.

questions.prompt: Two Sum (sorted array) two-pointer time complexity:
questions.explanation: l and r each move at most n times total. Two pointers, single pass.

questions.prompt: Two Sum on sorted [1,2,3,4,6], target=6. Pair:
questions.explanation: Indices 1 and 3 hold values 2 and 4 which sum to 6.

questions.prompt: Same-direction slow/fast pointers: slow marks:
choices: The boundary of the 'processed and valid' region — where the next accepted element should go
questions.explanation: Slow is the 'write cursor'. Fast is the 'read cursor' scanning for elements worth keeping.

questions.prompt: Remove duplicates from [1,1,2,3,3]. New length:
questions.explanation: Unique elements: 1,2,3 — length 3.

questions.prompt: Dutch National Flag algorithm maintains how many 'zones'?
questions.explanation: Zone 1: confirmed 0s [0..low-1]. Zone 2: confirmed 1s [low..mid-1]. Zone 3: unknown [mid..high]. Zone 4: confirmed 2s [high+1..n-1].

questions.prompt: Dutch Flag: after swap(nums, mid, high--), mid is NOT incremented because:
choices: The element swapped from high is unseen — it could be 0, 1, or 2 and must be examined next
It causes an infinite loop otherwise
questions.explanation: You just received an unknown element at mid from the right zone — you haven't processed it yet.

questions.prompt: Dutch Flag on [2,0,1]. Final array:
questions.explanation: Result is sorted [0,1,2].

questions.prompt: Palindrome check using two pointers:
choices: O(n) time O(n) space — copy reverse
O(n) time O(1) space — converging l and r
questions.explanation: l starts at 0, r at n-1. Compare and move inward — O(n) single pass, no extra array.

questions.prompt: Three Sum requires sorting first because:
choices: Sorting allows duplicate skipping and enables the inner converging two-pointer
It speeds up the O(n) part
questions.explanation: Sorted array lets you skip duplicate anchors (i) and duplicate pairs (l,r) cleanly to avoid repeat triplets.

questions.prompt: Three Sum time complexity after sorting:
questions.explanation: Outer loop O(n) × inner two-pointer O(n) = O(n²). Sorting is O(n log n) — dominated by O(n²).

questions.prompt: Interval merging: you sort by interval start because:
choices: Intervals that could overlap are adjacent after sorting — single forward scan suffices
It produces the output in order
questions.explanation: Sorting ensures the next interval either overlaps the current one (starts ≤ current end) or is definitively separate.

questions.prompt: Two intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap when:
choices: c <= b  (new interval starts before or when current interval ends)
questions.explanation: If c > b the intervals are disjoint. If c <= b they overlap and should merge to [a, max(b,d)].

questions.prompt: Merge [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]. Count of merged intervals:
questions.explanation: [1,6], [8,10], [15,18] — three non-overlapping merged intervals.

questions.prompt: Container With Most Water uses converging pointers because:
choices: Moving the shorter side inward is the only direction that can increase area — moving the taller side can only decrease it
questions.explanation: Area = min(height[l], height[r]) × (r-l). The width shrinks by 1 each step, so you must try to increase the height by moving the shorter side.

questions.prompt: Container With Most Water on heights [1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7]. Max area:
questions.explanation: Indices 1 and 8 with heights 8 and 7: area = 7 × 7 = 49.

questions.prompt: Move Zeroes in-place (keep relative order of non-zeroes) uses:
choices: Same-direction two-pointer: slow writes non-zeroes, fast scans
questions.explanation: slow pointer marks next write position; fast pointer finds non-zero elements to move there.

questions.prompt: Which problems require the array to be SORTED before applying two pointers?
questions.explanation: Remove duplicates already receives a sorted array (problem guarantee). Dutch Flag sorts via the algorithm itself — no pre-sort needed. Two Sum and Three Sum need intentional sorting.

questions.prompt: Trapping Rain Water optimal solution:
choices: O(n) two-pointer — l and r converge, tracking max heights from each side
O(n log n) sort then scan
Both A and B are O(n) — two-pointer uses O(1) space vs O(n)
questions.explanation: Prefix/suffix approach O(n) space. Two-pointer achieves same in O(1) space by processing the shorter column first.

questions.prompt: Two pointers fail (give wrong answer) on an unsorted array for two-sum because:
choices: Without sorted order, you cannot determine which pointer to move — a small left value paired with a small right value could match, but moving either pointer might skip the answer
Arrays must always be sorted in Java
questions.explanation: The 'move l right to increase sum, move r left to decrease sum' logic only holds when the array is monotonically non-decreasing.

flashcards.back: Start l=0, r=n-1 on SORTED array. Move l right if sum too small, r left if too large. O(n).
slow = write cursor (valid region boundary). fast = read cursor (scanner). O(n).
3 zones: 0s|1s|unknown|2s. mid scans unknown. Don't increment mid after swap with high.
Sort first. Fix i, then converge l and r. Skip duplicates at i, l, and r.
Sort by start. Merge if next.start <= current.end. Extend end to max of both ends.
l=0, r=n-1. Compare chars, move both inward. O(n) time O(1) space.
Move the SHORTER side inward — only way to potentially increase area.
Converging pattern REQUIRES sorted array. Same-direction works on unsorted too.

project.brief:
Write TwoPointersLab.java with:
1. twoSum(int[] sorted, int target) → int[]  (converging, sorted input)
2. removeDuplicates(int[] nums) → int  (in-place, sorted)
3. sortColors(int[] nums)  (Dutch National Flag, in-place)
4. threeSum(int[] nums) → List<List<Integer>>  (all unique triplets)
5. merge(int[][] intervals) → int[][]  (sorted merged intervals)
6. maxArea(int[] height) → int  (container with most water)
Include main() tests: all zeros, all same, no valid pair, single interval, already merged.

checklist.text: twoSum returns {-1,-1} when no pair found, not an exception
sortColors: mid NOT incremented after swap with high
threeSum skips duplicate i values AND duplicate l/r values after finding a triplet
merge handles single interval and already-sorted non-overlapping intervals

hints: sortColors: while(mid <= high) is the loop condition — not mid < high.
threeSum: after adding a triplet, advance both l++ and r-- then skip while duplicates.
merge: the overlap condition is intervals[i][0] <= last[1] — not strictly less than.
